summary: Stancil argues that elite VC firms increasingly prioritize being chosen over being good at picking, and brand has become the dominant lever for winning deals. In a world where reputations are built by viral attention and cultural signaling, firms hire for vibe and notoriety—even when the ethics are messy—because attention itself has become the business.
money_quotes: In other words, clever investors have to find the deal and win it; popular investors can partially outsource the picking, and focus more on the winning.
Reputation is the one thing that can’t be bought.
Now, reputations are created by poasting.
He was hired for the vibe—and today, vibes are business decisions.
The business model is inverted—the media company and popular podcasts aren’t about deal flow; they are the point.
key_insight: Venture capital has shifted from a quiet capital-allocation game to an attention-and-prestige business where brand, controversy, and cultural cachet drive hiring and deal access.
